data.title: If I Were Retired Today, These 3 Income Machines Would Be My First Buys
data.description:
Introduction

As some of you may know, I was a Corporate Treasury intern in the past. That was back in 2019 when I was still figuring out what I wanted to do in life. A few months after I left to finish my master’s degree in International Business Administration (with a focus on purchasing and supply chains), my former boss retired. That’s old news and doesn’t affect your portfolio at all. However, because Henkel (the company where I interned) is a European heavyweight, he was interviewed, as he was a rather powerful treasury manager.

I’m bringing that up because I just re-read the piece. One thing stood out to me:

Treasury chief Michael Reuter has left consumer-goods company Henkel and retired at the end of September, as DerTreasurer has learned. Shortly before his departure, he and his team were able to make a splash in the capital market: Henkel issued two sterling bonds totaling the equivalent of 850 million euros, both with a negative yield. - Der Treasurer (translated)

Again, this isn’t about Henkel or about my past. This is about the last line in that paragraph, which mentioned that Henkel issued the equivalent of EUR 850 million in negative-yielding debt. Back then, people paid corporations to take their money. Sure, Henkel is an A-rated giant with terrific diversification, but it’s still “nuts” if you think about it.

And, to use a chart from 2019, it wasn’t unusual. Back then, the total amount of debt with a negative yield was $16 trillion. More than 28% of the debt tracked by the Bloomberg Barclays Global Aggregate Index had a yield of less than 0%.

Back then, it was truly the best time to own low-risk, high-quality dividend growth stocks, as investors were aggressively chasing income as if the world would end. This was obviously fully supported by ultra-low rates in developed nations.

To me, it’s a perfect example of how major capital shifts impact our portfolios. Less than two years ago, when rates spiked, the exact opposite happened. Some investors (I’m painting with a broad brush again) sold equities and went into short-term bonds that yielded more than 5%. That explains the massive surge in money market assets, as we can see below.

While I am typing this, there’s close to $8 trillion in money market funds, which is basically short-term government debt (risk-free income, so to speak).

In 2020, that number was $5 trillion. Between 2010 and 2019, it was $3 trillion on a very consistent basis, as investors were buying income in other places, as the yield on short-term debt was close to zero.

These are the capital rotations I care so much about, as they are crucial for asset management. We’re seeing the same in equities. In the first two months of this year, investors wanted cyclical value stocks (that’s my core thesis, as most readers will know) and non-U.S. equities.

As we can see below, over the past 15 years, U.S. stocks were the place to be. However, on a year-to-date basis in 2026 (that’s January-February), U.S. stocks lagged international stocks.

The war in Iran changed that.

My friend and business partner, Albert Marko, wrote on that, as he made the case that the U.S. is using the conflict to create new capital flows into the U.S., based on the realization that in times of distress, it has the safest supply chains (think of its oil supply), dollar safety, military power, and other tailwinds.

Bloomberg just confirmed that:

But the developments since the US and Israeli attacks on Iran reveal that America is still the go-to market for investors. If the US has warts, it remains the center of global innovation and home to the deepest and most liquid markets on the planet, a feature that becomes indispensable when economic shocks hit. After 14 chaotic months, we’re also seeing signs emerge of institutional resilience at the Federal Reserve and Supreme Court, an additional source of comfort. - Bloomberg

That’s a good thing, as it not only supports my thesis that America remains a terrific (if not the best) market for long-term capital allocation, but also because the market’s biggest companies are now in need of massive funding. While most hyperscalers (think of the Mag-7) have terrific balance sheets, AI spending is now forcing them to diversify these risks.

It was just reported that Amazon (AMZN) is issuing $37 billion in bonds. According to Reuters, the bond deal resulted in $126 billion worth of demand, which is a good sign for Amazon. And then there’s Alphabet (GOOGL), which raised $100 billion, including through a 100-year bond. It was observed 10x, according to Seeking Alpha.

At this point, I have to admit that my intro seems to be all over the place (and close to 1,000 words - sorry!).

However, it brings me to my main point, which is that as global markets are getting volatile, the U.S. once again defeats the argument that it’s not the go-to place for capital anymore. We also see that Americans are using it to issue debt for the AI transition.

Unfortunately, this creates a bit of an issue. As rates are potentially falling, the environment isn’t great for income anymore. And while we’re far away from a 2019 scenario where it costs money in some cases to lend money to corporations, it’s not a scenario where I want to be forced into deals like financing the AI revolution. I want no part in that, at least not through bonds.

This brings me to the question that people ask me quite frequently, which is what I would own if I were retired right now? It’s high-quality U.S. income that comes with both income and unique characteristics that add value to most income portfolios.

With all of this in mind, let’s look at three income ideas I would buy today if I were retired.

Here’s What I Would Buy

A big part of the intro was about the credit market. I have often said that I do not like bonds, as I’m an “equity guy.” I want to own a share of a company and grow with it over time, while generating income, in some cases.

Right now, that lending market is under fire. While Alphabet and Amazon are not having a hard time finding buyers, the private credit market is seeing cracks. Many asset managers and their Business Development Companies have sold off hard this year, including some of the best players like Ares Management (ARES) and Apollo Global Management (APO), which I consider the gold standard of private credit.

Fears are basically created by software disruption and some negative headlines regarding “unexpected” defaults that make people wonder how much bad debt is hidden in this industry. When adding that private credit is very cyclical, it explains why I am so careful in this industry.

However, I still would buy exposure here, as there are some great deals out there. One of them is Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC). It’s the BDC owned by Ares Management. Right now, I am actually looking to buy ARES in the months ahead (I’ll update you on my liquidity and plans soon). However, if I were retired, I would buy the higher-yielding BDC.

Ares Capital currently yields 10.4%. This dividend hasn’t been cut since the Great Financial Crisis. And, as of December 31, 2025, it’s a BDC with a superior total return compared to banks and BDC peers, as Ares has figured out how to find a great balance between risk (yield) and safety (picking the right deals).

Moreover, not only is this the biggest BDC on the market, but also a BDC with terrific fundamentals, as it has a BBB credit rating from all three major rating agencies (Fitch even has a positive outlook, which could mean a path to BBB+), and more than $6 billion in liquidity.

Its portfolio has a nonaccrual rate of 1.8% (at cost). That’s in line with prior year levels, according to the company. Moreover, it’s below its own long-term average of 2.8% and below the BDC industry average of 3.8%. At fair value, that number is just 1.2%.

The company also believes its dividend is sustainable:

We believe ARCC is in a good position to maintain its dividend despite market expectations for further declines in short-term interest rates. We generally set our dividend level based on our view of the earnings power of our company. While lower short-term rates present an earnings headwind, we believe there are multiple factors that can support our earnings and thus, our current dividend level for the foreseeable future. - ARCC 4Q25 Earnings Call

And to incorporate higher risks, ARCC is now being traded at 7% below book value.

Another stock I would buy is Agree Realty Series A (ADC.PR.A), which is Agree Realty’s preferred stock. In other words, it’s somewhat of a hybrid between a bond and a stock. Investors get exposure to Agree Realty (equity ownership), yet no voting rights and no dividend growth. What they do get is bond-like dividend payments and more safety, as preferred stock is more “senior” than common equity.

Generally speaking, I dislike capped upside, which applies to assets without dividend growth. However, the risk/reward of this preferred stock is great, as it trades at $17.19 while I am writing this. That’s substantially lower than the liquidation price of $25. That’s the price you’ll get if the company were to buy back the preferred stock.

That limited upside isn’t great for long-term growth investors, yet it’s perfect for income, as it is a premium of 45% compared to the current price. That’s the upside you’ll get before you potentially lose these shares in a buyback. Moreover, because of the low price, the monthly dividend yield is 6.2%. That’s a terrific yield, roughly 200 basis points above the 10-year government bond.

As a comparison, Agree Realty (ADC) common stock yields 3.9%. That dividend has a five-year CAGR of 1.9%. On October 14, it raised the dividend by 2.3%. If we assume that the dividend growth rate holds, investors will end up with a yield on cost of 4.9% after ten years, which is still way below the rate on the preferred stock.

It also helps that Agree has an A-rated balance sheet, a portfolio that mostly caters to ultra-safe net lease tenants, and growth opportunities in areas like sale-leaseback. Moreover, as this preferred stock is cumulative, if Agree were to run into issues leading to dividend cuts, it would have to make preferred shareholders whole before it would be allowed to pay a common dividend again.

I really like this preferred stock. And, if I were to retire today, I would buy this in a heartbeat.

The third pick is somewhat unusual, as it’s Rayonier (RYN). This company is a specialty REIT. Technically, it’s a “Land Resources REIT,” as it owns timberland that covers more than 4 million acres after merging with PotlatchDeltic.

As a result, their business model is based on three factors. They sell timber to lumber mills, where lumber for a wide range of purposes is created. Homebuilding is a major factor. This business is cyclical, as it depends on pricing and demand. It also generates high-quality revenue from strategic master-planned communities. That’s high-quality developed land for new housing communities. It’s much less volatile than selling timber.

Last but not least, they use land for value-adding opportunities like solar, carbon credits, bioenergy, and so much more.

As we can see in the total return chart below (capital gains + dividends), RYN isn’t a low-volatility stock, which is why I have always avoided it. If I want a volatile stock, I prefer buying a housing supplier that tends to rise faster during times of strong economic growth.

The good news is that the RYN risk/reward looks highly attractive.

As we can see below, despite the increase in the ISM Manufacturing Index (the black line), the year-over-year performance of RYN has continued to go down. At this point, I like the risk/reward a lot.

If we get broadening economic growth, we’ll see both pricing and demand tailwinds in this space. Moreover, while the dividend was cut by 4.6% in February, it was part of the merger. Currently, it yields 5.2% based on a quarterly dividend of $0.27.

At points like these, I think RYN makes for a great investment that provides income, a great risk/reward, as well as inflation protection for an income portfolio, as this stock tends to do well in times of rising inflation.

Generally speaking, I truly believe that all three of these picks bring something truly unique to the table that I find highly compelling for an income portfolio. And, as I always say, stay tuned for more ideas!

For now, here’s a short takeaway:

Takeaway

My introduction was pretty chaotic today. However, my point is that it’s all about identifying capital rotations and finding the best risk/reward for an income portfolio, or any portfolio, really.

At a time when money market funds are overflowing with capital, rates are likely to be pressured, and economic growth is set to rebound, I like to apply a diversified approach to buying high-quality income.

If I were retired today, I would buy ARCC for elevated BDC income, preferred Agree Realty stock due to a 6% yield and a terrific risk/reward, and Rayonier because it provides 5% income and cyclical tailwinds and elevated inflation protection.

These are three different themes, but all have one thing in common, which is a unique ability to add value to an income portfolio.

Three Risks You Need To Know

  • The biggest risk for ARCC is credit risk. While it is protected against further declines in short-term rates, a domino effect in credit could lead to elevated non-accrual rates.
  • For Agree Realty’s preferred stock, the biggest risk is a surge in long-term government debt rates. As these compete with preferred stock due to the bond-like dividend payments, they could keep a lid on capital gains.
  • For RYN, the biggest risk is a sluggish housing market and related pricing headwinds in lumber.

data.title: REITs May Be The Biggest Winner Of The Coming Market Shift
data.description:
Over the past years, we have had many different narratives dominate market headlines and influence the valuations of various sectors.

First, we had the pandemic, which massively favored tech stocks but hurt real-world industries like real estate and entertainment.

Then, we had the historic surge in inflation and interest rates, which again favored tech stocks (QQQ; SPY) and hurt more traditional yield-oriented sectors like MLPs (AMLP), REITs (VNQ), Utilities (XLU), etc.

But I think that the tables are finally turning as we are now in for a new, even more powerful narrative that will reshape the markets over the coming years.

I think that most equities will suffer, even as REITs will emerge as the next big winner, and if you give me 5 minutes of your time, I will explain to you why that is.

AI is the new hot topic, and rightfully so. While many investors seem to think that "AI is overhyped", I still think that it is the opposite. I believe that it is the most disruptive technological innovation of the century, and yet, 84% of people have yet to even use it:

I predicted almost a year ago that as AI models get better over time and more people start using them, it would eventually disrupt lots of businesses, and the market is now finally starting to recognize this risk.

It started with SaaS (Software as a Service) companies (CRM; CNSWF), which have now crashed by 30-50%, almost entirely due to AI breaking barriers to entry, and making it far easier than ever before for new companies to build cheaper alternatives and compete in this space:

But that was just the beginning.

The market is now slowly realizing that SaaS companies are just the tip of the iceberg.

I have previously argued that AI will hurt most businesses and could lead to a broader stock market crash, and I still believe that.

Professional service providers are next in line. Think of consulting companies, marketing agencies, law firms, real estate brokers, engineering firms, etc. Lots of such businesses are publicly listed, and they are getting disrupted as AI makes intelligence far cheaper and broadly available. The world's biggest real estate consulting firm, CBRE (CBRE), just crashed for that exact reason, despite posting strong quarterly results:

And just in case you don't believe me, these are the headlines on Google: It is all about how AI is set to disrupt its business.

Next are all transportation-related businesses, and there are lots of them. Think of trucking companies, logistics firms, delivery, taxis, buses, car manufacturers, gas stations, convenience stores, car insurance companies, lenders financing cars, parking, etc. They will suffer as electric autonomous vehicles greatly reduce the cost of transportation and reduce the need to own a personal car. Lots of major car manufacturers have already suffered from BYD (BYDDF) and Tesla (TSLA) disrupting the car industry, but these are still early days:

Traditional manufacturing businesses will also face severe disruption as AI, robotics, and eventually humanoids disrupt the landscape. Some will likely win big, even as those companies that are too slow to adapt lose big.

Healthcare will also change from top to bottom, as AI can now already answer lots of health-related questions, analyze scans, and even perform surgeries with great precision. Some of this tech is not yet deployed, but it will be over time, and the impact on healthcare providers will be significant.

Office landlords like SL Green (SLG) will also suffer as AI eliminates lots of white-collar jobs, likely leading to higher vacancy rates, lower rents, and more difficulties refinancing these buildings. This is particularly true as tenants will become reluctant to sign new leases if they don't know how many employees they will need in just a few years from now...

If what I said earlier about SaaS businesses, professional service providers, logistics firms, manufacturers, healthcare, and offices is true, then lots of lenders like BDCs (BIZD) and asset managers (GPZ) will also face severe pain as they hold large investments in these sectors. This is already reflected in their share prices, which have crashed due to these fears in the past months:

My point here is that most businesses are likely to suffer over time from AI.

Those that are truly immune to it are the exception. Even things that may seem well-protected, such as a plumbing company, could suffer over time, as more people decide to go into "AI-safe" trades, leading to oversupply and more competition. At the same time, the demand for these services may decline as DIY self-fixes become the norm for smaller issues, thanks to the assistance of AI. Finally, starting a tiny solo plumbing company is now far easier than ever before, with AI handling all your marketing, ads, website design, accounting, customer service, etc. More supply, less demand, lower profits.

So my point here is that very few businesses are truly safe from the deflationary forces of AI.

SaaS businesses were just the first domino, and as the market realizes this, I would expect a lot more stocks to drop to materially lower levels as investors reevaluate their models, reducing long-term growth rates and increasing their discount rates to account for this uncertainty.

As people then sell a lot of these stocks, I expect a historic capital rotation to occur towards AI-resistant asset classes, and I cannot think of anything better than REITs in this context.

Real estate investment trusts, or REITs (VNQ) in short, are publicly listed real estate investment vehicles that primarily own recession- and AI-resistant property types like:

  • Data centers: Digital Realty (DLR)
  • Cell towers: American Tower (AMT)
  • Skilled nursing facilities (OHI)
  • Senior housing (WELL)
  • Hospitals (MPT)
  • Net lease properties (O)
  • Grocery stores (REG)
  • Timberland (WY)
  • Farmland (FPI)
  • Apartment communities (AVB)
  • Single-family homes (AMH)
  • Manufactured housing communities (ELS)
  • E-commerce warehouses (PLD)
  • Distribution centers (PLD)
  • Cold storage (COLD)
  • Self-storage (PSA)
  • Etc.

These real assets are absolutely essential to the survival and prosperity of the human race, and yet, they are strictly limited in supply in their locations, building permits, construction costs, and the availability and cost of financing.

And AI cannot change that.

It cannot remove the need to have a roof over your head or the need to store goods in a warehouse to protect them from the elements.

It also cannot just magically create more land, rewrite zoning, grant new building permits in already built-out desirable locations, provide construction materials, or finance new development projects.

These barriers will always remain and protect existing buildings from new competitors, especially when located in built-out locations with growing demand.

Therefore, REITs are the ultimate anti-AI asset class. They are the exact opposite of anything "artificial" in that they own "real" assets that cannot be easily disrupted.

And as more investors come to this same realization, I would expect a lot more capital to flow towards anti-AI asset classes like REITs, potentially pushing their share prices to a lot higher levels.

You don't have to take it just from me.

This is already happening: REITs clearly benefited from capital flowing from AI-disrupted businesses like SaaS companies into them in early 2026 as they surged in value even as large pockets of the market collapsed:

My prediction is simply that this is still just the beginning, and as AI models only become better and start to disrupt other industries, we will see capital rotate from AI-disrupted to AI-resilient sectors on a much larger scale, turning REITs into the biggest winners of this next market shift.

We are betting heavily on that at High Yield Landlord and expect our portfolio to surge in value over the coming years as this prediction comes to life.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: DIAX: Merger Approved, Composition About To Change
data.description:
Thesis

We covered the Nuveen Dow 30 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (DIAX) last year when we highlighted for investors a potential corporate action in respect to the closed-end fund. At the time, the Board of Directors for the CEF had approved a merger with other Nuveen funds:

CHICAGO, September 19, 2025--(BUSINESS WIRE)--The Boards of Trustees of Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund (BXMX), Nuveen Dow 30℠ Dynamic Overwrite Fund (DIAX), and Nuveen S&P 500 Dynamic Overwrite Fund (SPXX) have approved a proposal to merge the funds. The proposed mergers, if approved by shareholders, would combine BXMX and DIAX into SPXX. The mergers are intended to create a larger fund with lower net operating expenses and increased trading volume on the exchange for common shares.

The first step in a merger is for the Board of Directors to approve the corporate action, followed by a shareholder vote. If the shareholders do not think the corporate action is favorable and do not approve it, then it falls through. In our case, the outcome was positive.

In today's article we are going to discuss the result of the shareholder vote and the next steps for DIAX and its holders.

The merger was approved

The shareholders of the three involved CEFs have approved the merger:

Shareholders of Nuveen S&P 500 Buy-Write Income Fund, Nuveen Dow 30 Dynamic Overwrite Fund, and Nuveen S&P 500 Dynamic Overwrite Fund have approved a proposal to merge the funds. The mergers will combine each of BXMX and DIAX into SPXX. Subject to the satisfaction of certain customary closing conditions, the mergers are expected to become effective before the market opens on March 30, 2026.

As per the fund communication, this translates into SPXX becoming the 'surviving' fund. This means that on March 30, 2026 DIAX will cease to exist, and shareholders will see in their brokerage account SPXX shares. The merger will be done on a NAV basis, meaning investors will receive an equivalent NAV basis of shares of SPXX. Doing a NAV-to-NAV merger eliminates the issues created by different discounts to NAV for CEFs:

As we can see from the above chart, DIAX was perceived as a poor CEF by the market, and thus has traded historically at very deep discounts to NAV. Conversely, SPXX was better traded, with a price close to NAV in mid 2025. The two CEFs have seen a convergence of discounts due to the merger put forward. Basically, if DIAX were much wider and the merger had been approved, market participants could have done an arbitrage trade where they would have bought DIAX at a large discount to get converted at a better price into SPXX.

The transition to the new ticker and the mechanics are the easy part since they are Nuveen's responsibility. What matters for investors, though, is the fund direction and risk factors. Let us take a look at that.

The new fund will have a different mandate than DIAX

As we have written before about DIAX and as we can see from the fund website in terms of objective, the CEF:

Is designed to offer regular distributions through a strategy that seeks attractive total return with less volatility than the Dow Jones Industrial Average (DJIA or "Dow30") by investing in an equity portfolio that seeks to substantially replicate the price movements of the DJIA, as well as selling call options on 35%-75% of the notional value of the Fund's equity portfolio (with a 55% long-term target) in an effort to enhance the Fund's risk-adjusted returns.

DIAX is a fund that uses a covered call strategy to replicate the Dow Jones long term returns. Conversely, SPXX is an S&P 500 covered call fund. The index will thus change, as so will the risk-reward. The S&P 500 has more technology in its composition, and a different volatility and performance status. So what should investors do?

What should investors do?

Investors need to realize they will no longer have Dow Jones exposure via the new fund and thus act accordingly. If they still desire a covered call fund with Dow Jones exposure, they can look at the Global X Dow 30 Covered Call ETF (DJIA) which we covered here.

In terms of S&P 500 covered call exposure, we are not big fans of SPXX since it has underperformed other choices in the space:

The clear underperformer from the cohort is the JPMorgan Equity Premium Income ETF (JEPI), but SPXX is the next underperformer. The outstanding names here are the Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund (EOI) and the Goldman Sachs S&P 500 Premium Income ETF (GPIX).

So we are neutral for SPXX, highlighting, though, that it is not among the best in the space for covered call S&P 500 exposure.

If investors are fine with having a different index as exposure, then they do not need to do anything - they will see in the brokerage account the new ticker into April 2026. Conversely, investors wanting to get back Dow Jones exposure should wait for the merger to be complete and see if the discount narrows a bit from here. We anticipate it will, since SPXX has traded better historically and it will move away from the current uncertainty aspect. After a slight tightening in discounts (think 1% to 4%), one can sell SPXX and buy Dow Jones outright or DJIA as a covered call fund.

Conclusion

DIAX is a covered call fund from Nuveen. The name is set to be merged into SPXX at the end of March 2026, with the merger now fully approved by all sets of shareholders (after the Board of Directors approval). The merger will result in the holders changing exposure completely, with the surviving fund, SPXX, being an S&P 500 covered call fund. We expect a slight narrowing of the CEF discount post merger, but a performance for the new fund in line with what it has posted in the past. Investors comfortable with S&P 500 exposure do not need to do anything, while investors who still want Dow Jones risk factors should sell post-merger and look at DIA or DJIA.

data.title: AMD's Next AI Catalyst: MI450
data.description:
Investment Thesis

AMD (AMD) is still misunderstood and broadly perceived as a strong CPU player and a secondary player to Nvidia (NVDA) in the GPU space. I still think that is an incorrect way to look at it. The AI infrastructure is growing from a training-only model to a systems/inference model that is less dependent on a software stack. AMD has a strong chance to grow with its record revenue of $34.6 billion in 2025 with its hyperscaler partners OpenAI (OPENAI), Meta, and Oracle with the MI450. AMD is down 10% since my last coverage, but the outlook has only gotten stronger.

AMD's Hidden Advantage: The CPU-GPU Data Center Flywheel

Investors are concerned about the opportunity for AMD GPUs. This is a valid concern. The Data Center opportunity for AMD is misunderstood by investors. The Data Center opportunity for AMD is driven by a flywheel effect of two engines: EPYC server CPUs, which are growing server CPU share over time, and Instinct GPUs, which are now becoming relevant to hyperscalers. The fact that AI computing clusters are not built around GPUs is not true. The fact that CPUs are required for orchestration, head nodes, storage, and increasingly agentic computing is not true.

This is reinforced by the management commentary. On the Q4 call, Lisa Su said server CPU demand remained strong even in a seasonally weak quarter. Further, the company expects Q1’26 server CPU revenue to increase sequentially into Q1. Additionally, there is very high customer demand for the upcoming Venice platform. This is important to note since it suggests the company’s EPYC products are not just holding their own against the competition but are actually gaining traction ahead of the company’s MI450 GPU product cycle. This is important since the company is launching its biggest product cycle in accelerators while its CPUs are strong and generating significant revenue.

The financials suggest the company is improving its operating leverage. On the call, the company’s CFO Jean Hu, said the company increased its inventory levels by $607 million sequentially to $7.9 billion to support the strong data center demand. However, the company still managed to deliver a gross margin of 57%. This is close to 55%, excluding one-time items related to China. This suggests the margin structure is improving even as the company increases its top line. This suggests to me the company is increasing its margin products rather than just increasing volume.

Why AMD’s Biggest Opportunity Isn’t Training

If there is one part of the AMD investment thesis where I think the opportunity still may not be appreciated to the fullest, it is the inference opportunity. Training was the one place where Nvidia excelled since the ecosystem benefits those companies that can deliver the tight integration, scale, and software moat developed over time.

However, I think the inference opportunity is where the asymmetry between the two companies really comes to the forefront. Inference is a very different market. It is more fragmented. It is more cost-sensitive. It is more memory bandwidth, latency, throughput, and cost of ownership sensitive. This is not to say the door isn’t shut on Nvidia. I just think the door may be a little ajar.

AMD’s own positioning has reflected this shift. They have repeatedly talked about distributed inference, enterprise AI and rack-scale solutions rather than just silicon. The MI450 launch is expected to start in the second half of 2026. The impact will be more in Q4 and 2027. Su called this an inflection point. They are clearly investing in a platform that’s a full rack-scale solution rather than just a chip. Oracle, OpenAI, and Meta are not buying a lab experiment. They’re buying a system-level platform.

AMD is progressing well, though they are still lagging behind Nvidia in the areas that are most important. The MI355X GPU has good capabilities in hardware performance (red line below) and might be competitive for simple inference workloads. It even shows competitive performance/cost for some workloads, which suggests that AMD hardware is getting more and more capable.

Looking forward, the MI400 family may prove to be the more significant milestone. With rack-scale Helios solutions (MI455X) announced for the second half of 2026, AMD is shifting from selling individual accelerators to competing with complete AI solutions, like Nvidia's rack-scale solutions. On paper, the MI400 architecture and memory provide attractive inference economics.

That is where I think a lot of investors still miss the upside. The market is just viewing the AMD AI opportunity as a straight-line extension of MI300 and MI350. And then it discounts it because Nvidia is the incumbent. But MI450 is a platform reset.

Why AMD’s Valuation Looks Expensive, Until You Look Forward

If you look at AMD today, the current price does not look cheap based on trailing numbers. The current trailing P/E is 75x. That is a number that a value investor does not want to use as a reference. But it is also a number that does not make a lot of sense as a reference for a company whose earnings power is still being rebased.

The better way to think about it is forward normalization. If AMD is able to get closer to the framework that was presented at Analyst Day, particularly the combination of greater than 35% revenue CAGR along with EPS greater than $20 in that strategic timeframe, then the price starts to make a lot more sense in terms of being on the way to a much larger earnings base.

Officially, AMD's targets are greater than 60% data center revenue CAGR along with greater than 35% non-GAAP operating margins over the longer term. These are not easy targets. This is a company that's going to be a lot more profitable over time due to the increasing mix of artificial intelligence and servers.

Therefore, the valuation of AMD appears high based on trailing numbers but rapidly compresses based on forward earnings. EPS growth is expected to accelerate from 2026’s $6.64 to 2028’s $14.45, along with revenue growth from 2026’s $46.5 billion to 2028’s $85 billion, implying a forward P/E of ~29x to ~13x, respectively.

The Real Challenge Ahead

The biggest risk remains the execution on the MI450 and Helios. AMD is not just moving from selling accelerators to selling AI systems on a rack scale. This is a much larger scale. This is a much tougher challenge. Lisa Su sounded very confident that the ramp ahead would not be supply-constrained. She said they have planned the build-out to the component level. I think this is good news. The industry has shown us how hard a rack-scale AI deployment can be when one element of the stack is not keeping pace.

There is the software question. ROCm is clearly improved. Open source is a big plus. But one can achieve good enough on a given deployment. This is not the same as closing the overall gap versus CUDA. This is the entire enterprise software stack. AMD does not need to achieve software parity, but they do need to achieve a level of maturity on the right workloads so software is not the default objection every time they are evaluated.

Takeaway

I see AMD has passed the credible alternative phase, becoming a second platform in AI infrastructure just as inference is becoming more important. If the management team can execute on the MI450 and Helios ramp, today’s valuation is very conservative.

data.title: Sable Offshore: The Tides Have Shifted In Favor Of Production Start (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Sable Offshore (SOC) has received substantial support at the federal level in recent months in an attempt to bring back online the Santa Ynez Unit, a major Pacific offshore project that is expected to produce roughly 60,000 boe/d once fully ramped up. With events unfolding in the Middle East, oil prices are reacting in a big way, sending WTI and Brent Crude upwards of $120/bbl before settling closer to the $95-105/bbl range, creating urgency by the federal government to bolster production in an attempt to offset stranded barrels. On March 13, 2026, the Trump Administration approved Sable to resume production on its offshore rig, a major win for the company following years of delays due to local politics. Given the production outlook of Sable’s offshore platform coming online in 2026, I am upgrading SOC shares to a Strong Buy rating with a price target of $31/share.

International gas prices have experienced similar pricing pressure following the start of the events in the Middle East, sending the Dutch and UK benchmarks to recent highs last seen at the start of 2025, while US prices remain relatively subdued due to the abundance of local supply and limited external exposure. Early on in the events, QatarEnergy halted LNG output, which accounts for 20% of the global LNG export market.

The crux of the matter is the bottleneck in the Strait of Hormuz, which transports roughly 20% of the global oil consumption, or 20MMbbl/d.

In an attempt to offset stranded barrels and stabilize the global oil economy, the International Energy Agency’s [IEA] 32 member countries have agreed to the releasing of 400MMbbl from their respective strategic petroleum reserves [SPRs], with the US targeting 172MMbbl. Accordingly, the oil release is only expected to offset 15MMbbl/d of net supply loss of crude and refined products, potentially only remediating a small proportion of the problem. Assuming 20MMbbl/d is released from the global SPR, the initiative will only support 20 days of supply, providing only limited alleviation from the matter.

In addition to this, President Trump announced a “strategic waiver” for countries to import Russian oil “stranded at sea.” According to the announcement, oil already in transit is the aim for the waiver and is not expected to support full production given the President’s attempts to maintain pressure on Russia’s economy. Similar to the SPR release, I believe that this is only a short-term fix and that it will have no meaningful impact on global oil prices.

As a result of the challenges at hand, President Trump has been fielding further development of the domestic production market, which I believe will take more creativity as the Iranian war isn’t expected to be a long-term operation. From a strategic standpoint, supporting shale oil production can provide support for the oil market given the short-cycle production and sharp decline curve relative to offshore production, providing producers like Exxon Mobil (XOM) flexibility in bringing wells online or pulling back on production.

Taking the initiative, President Trump summoned authorities under the Defense Production Act in order to get Sable’s offshore production on stream again on March 13, 2026. This isn’t necessarily the first time the federal government has attempted to invoke its power to bring the offshore platform’s production back online, as this has been a multiyear legal challenge for Sable imposed by the State of California and local government and related agencies.

Strategically, the Pacific Coast can be an ideal region for gas production to be shipped overseas to the Asian market, providing an appealing opportunity for Sable’s gas production.

While the organization is weighing its two options for direct export off the platform using an offshore storage & treating [OS&T] vessel or a restart of the Santa Ynez Pipeline System for local refining, the events unfolding in real time could present an opportunity to favor maintaining domestic refining as opposed to international export of oil production. While I believe the financial case for the OS&T vessel is significantly more appealing, the circumstances of war can result in local offtake to alleviate the local economy from high oil prices.

While this is an appealing argument, 60Mboe/d hardly scratches the surface of the 20MMbbl/d of stranded oil in the Middle East. In fact, assuming a full stream of 60Mboe/d and the historical production mix of 86.5% oil, Sable will only be producing roughly 52Mbbl/d, and this is at the expected peak of production following a ramp-up period.

Of the two strategies, Sable can spin up production using the Las Flores Canyon & Pipeline System as soon as approval is received. The OS&T vessel option is estimated to achieve first sale by q4’26.

Sable Offshore Financial Outlook & Valuation

For forecasting purposes, I’m using Brent oil futures and Henry Hub gas futures quoted on the CME Group website.

A critical difference between my last report covering SOC and this report is the operating leverage enabled by higher expected oil prices for the coming years before normalizing back to the $70/bbl range.

Using these assumptions in the model, we can expect SOC shares to trade at roughly $31/share based on the present value of future cash flows [using EBITDA as a proxy for cash flow]. Given the appealing market environment and the urgency for the US to bolster production in order to offset rising oil prices, I believe the case for Sable to restart production is quickly becoming more likely. I am upgrading SOC shares to a Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Coca-Cola: A Wonderful Business But Limited Return Expectation
data.description:
Coca-Cola (KO) remains one of the strongest consumer franchises globally. However, at ~25x earnings and with structural growth likely limited to mid-single digits, the expected shareholder return appears capped near 8–10%. While the business quality is exceptional, the current valuation leaves little margin of safety. At the current share price of $77-78, KO does not present a compelling buy proposition for fresh portfolio allocation for the time being.

Introduction—Business Quality and Growth Dynamics

The name Coca-Cola hardly needs any introduction either to consumers as a product or to investors as a listed equity. The stock currently trades at a price-to-earnings multiple of around 25x (TTM) basis. On a standalone basis, a 25x multiple does not appear particularly expensive in today’s markets, especially for a business of the quality of Coca-Cola.

The company generated more than $13 billion in earnings for FY25 on an opening equity base of about $25 billion, implying a very impressive return on equity profile. In addition, this figure is based on unadjusted accounting book value. Just to reflect on that, the balance sheet contains around $15 billion in goodwill alone, apart from other intangible assets.

A business producing such high returns on capital might ordinarily command a valuation far more than a 25x earnings multiple.

However, this is where it becomes interesting. While Coca-Cola continues to generate high returns on its existing capital base, the company is unable to reinvest incremental earnings at similar rates of return. As a result, a large portion of profits needs to be distributed either as dividends or utilized in buybacks rather than compounded internally.

What matters is not merely the absolute dividend paid, but the portion of earnings required to fund it. In the case of KO, the dividend payout is around 65%. Thereby, a meaningful part of the shareholder return in the form of dividends resembles more of an annuity-like income stream rather than internally compounded growth. Therefore, even the 25x multiple of KO is not compelling because the company cannot utilize incremental capital at similar returns. In addition, considering the current scale of the company—around $50 billion in revenues and operations across the globe—it is nothing new to state that the growth rate of the company would broadly resemble overall economic prospects and would be in a fairly moderate range

Business Outlook

The company reiterated an organic (non-GAAP) revenue growth guidance of around 4–5% and a comparable EPS growth guidance of about 7–8% for FY 26. I do not find any fault with that guidance. However, at the same time, I do not consider what the company might guide for the next year more important than what it has done over a period of the last preceding years. A $50 billion business would naturally show gradual moderation in growth rather than acceleration.

Over the last decade, EPS has grown roughly 5–6% annually, and it is more reasonable to anchor long-term expectations closer to that range.

Also, the regional competition from new incumbents cannot be entirely ignored. In many developing markets, technology-driven distribution models and new-age marketing techniques may sometimes benefit newer players more than established ones such as Coca-Cola.

In addition, revenue growth in FY25 for the full financial year was around 2%, as per GAAP figures. Even the non-GAAP EPS growth for FY25, which the company reported at around 5%, was largely the result of price increases rather than volume expansion. Put simply, the quality of earnings may appear somewhat moderated when revenue growth is primarily driven by price increases rather than meaningful volume expansion.

This is not to suggest that there is anything fundamentally wrong with the business. The company continues to operate very well as a global franchise. The above points are merely observations that support a hold case going forward. In a nutshell, any optimism should remain measured, particularly considering the current valuation, the performance record over many years, and realistic forward expectations.

One other noteworthy aspect, which cannot be quantified with any probabilistic reasoning at present, relates to the health and ESG considerations directly associated with the nature of the business. Critics have long raised concerns about the health implications of sugar consumption, particularly in products such as Coca-Cola. Nevertheless, the company has continued to grow despite such criticism over many decades.

However, consumer behavior today appears less predictable than in previous generations. Preferences, tastes, and opinions tend to change more rapidly, particularly in an era of digitally interconnected consumers and fast-moving public narratives. In such an environment, campaigns—whether launched in good faith or with ulterior motives—can spread rapidly and potentially influence consumer perceptions significantly. These contingent factors affect both the company’s operations and potentially contribute to valuation re-rating pressures in the market.

Over the last five years, both dividends and revenues have grown at roughly 5–5.5% annually, while GAAP EPS has increased at around 6%.

Valuation makes it a hold

This is the only factor that makes KO rated as a hold for the time being rather than a buy. The market has priced KO very efficiently. With an expected earnings growth equal to historical ten-year EPS growth, which was around 5%—at a current multiple of 25x, there isn’t any margin of safety.

As it is also reflected in the above charts, the current price-to-earnings ratio is largely identical to what it has been over the last several years for KO. We can exclude the sudden spike appearing in the chart, which was attributed to an exceptional income tax write-down related to U.S. tax reform.

Therefore, over the last 10 years, total returns for KO have been around 9% on an annualized basis, which is quite identical with the EPS growth rate for that period plus dividend returns.

The point of drawing this comparison is not to suggest that KO had underdelivered or that the stock is overvalued in any sense. Although KO still has underperformed the S&P 500 considerably over the last decade. The purpose of the comparison nonetheless is simply to reflect that the current price does not offer meaningful potential for either near-term or structural market re-rating. Without such a re-rating, the total return over any medium- to long-term period should be more or less equal to the earnings growth rate plus dividend income

The dividend yield at the current price is around 2.75%-3%. As a working proxy, even if we incorporate a 5% per annum earnings growth rate along with a 5% per annum dividend growth rate, an investor would likely earn around 8–10% pre-tax XIRR over a 10-year period, assuming the identical earnings multiple of 25x. The EPS estimate eventually matches Seeking Alpha's estimate at the time of estimating terminal value. The projections in this case are not entirely on the conservative side, and no allowance is provided for the PE compression. This is done, since the analysis is not intended to support a buy thesis. This should not be considered as a proxy for a buy case, either in this instance or in any other context.

This is again broadly identical to the returns generated by KO over the previous 10-year period. I would not call them disappointing, but unless you are someone who has the problem of having more capital than investable ideas, this might still be a buy. Leaving aside this group of investors, I do not think it makes much meaningful sense as a fresh purchase at the current price. An 8%-9% pre-tax expected IRR does not look really appealing.

The relative comparison of KO with its peer group signals a moderate overvaluation as well. The peer group comparison is only relevant insofar as it helps identify whether there is any significant relative mispricing that may warrant attention. That is not the case at present.

Further, Seeking Alpha's quant rightly rates the valuation as D. In a nutshell, by whatever metric one might choose to analyze the company, there isn't a compelling investment proposition, unless someone would want to bet on a market re-rating—that is, the market assigning an even higher multiple to the company than it does currently. I believe there are far better propositions to bet on than a PE expansion in KO.

No Material Re-Rating Trigger—Catalysts are too small to move the needle

The market usually values a company higher when and if, it recognizes any significant trigger for the entity that has the potential to materially increase the overall business prospects of the company. Now Coca-Cola, being involved in numerous countries, does not lack such trigger events. Some interesting developments keep happening here and there. But, contrary to the view of many analysts, those developments are largely insignificant to really make a meaningful difference in the overall business performance. Also, a lot of these developments rather help the company mitigate adverse developments occurring in other regions.

Therefore, I would say that it is best to value or analyze a company like KO in terms of the structural business fundamentals it possesses at a particular point in time and how much we as investors are paying to participate in those economics. Being overly optimistic about small incremental developments would neither move the needle nor make a compelling case for the investment.

Takeaway

KO is not a compelling buy at a current price of $77. As a business, it is one of the great global franchises. But paying 25x earnings might not offer an investor any meaningful alpha. Even without speaking of the uncertainty inherent in any business, the market generously values KO. And frankly, we do not have to disagree with Mr. Market over the current valuation it has for the business. KO deserves that, and I do not yet believe there is any reason to dispose of the holding for a long-term investor of the company. They may continue to hold, unless they have a need for money or have an alternative proposition that is far more compelling.

The only reflection is that KO, at least as of now, does not offer any buy case for fresh investments. If we see any meaningful price correction of over 10-15% in the near future, we might want to revisit the thesis. And the greater the decline, the more compelling Coca-Cola would gradually become for a long-term investor. Seeking Alpha's quant rates it as 3.37 and assigns a hold. I have to agree with that assessment.

To conclude, KO does not offer much alpha for investors capable of independent security/company analysis. In the present market environment, there are other businesses, and even ETFs where the probability of achieving superior returns appears materially higher than KO.

data.title: Procter & Gamble: Uptick Likely Despite Risks
data.description:
It has been a while since I last wrote about the consumer goods giant Procter & Gamble (PG). That was in October 2023. Here's what struck me when I went back to that article in preparation for this one. The stock is up by just 5% since. For context, the S&P 500 (SP500) has seen over 10x that increase during the time (see chart below). P&G lags even the consumer staples sector considerably. The State Street Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Inc. ETF (XLP) is up by 27% since.

Why has the stock underperformed?

To be fair, PG's price hasn't been just weak since. Between October 2023 and December 2024, it had risen by over 16%. This is despite the fact that in Q1 FY24 (quarter ending September 2023, financial year ending June 2024), the company's numbers had weakened. Still, it kept going with momentum as core EPS growth had impressed in FY24 (see Slide 1 below), and the company expected a healthy 5-7% core EPS rise in FY25 as well.

But the earnings growth spurt was short-lived, and in FY25 core EPS growth undershot targets, growing by just 4%. The number's growth softened even further to just 1% by H1 FY26, no thanks to slowing sales growth (see Slide 2 below) below the company's five-year average levels of 3.4%. The stock price dropped in tandem, losing 14% of its value in 2025.

But don't write PG off just yet. Not with uncertainty returning to the stock markets. While the S&P 500 has gone nowhere YTD, the XLP is up by ~9% YTD and even as PG lags staples, it has risen by 5%.

Why P&G's sales could withstand challenges

Sure, there are risks to the stock stemming from the potential of further softening in sales and earnings growth as the crude oil price spike threatens to spill over into overall inflation. But even as these challenges exist, there are balancing factors too.

Sales' vulnerability to macro conditions...

Let's first get the discussion on the challenge to sales out of the way. There's a possibility that P&G is particularly vulnerable to macroeconomic conditions, going by its weak performance in H1 FY26, as noted earlier. Sales grew by just 1% YoY, as Q2 FY26 sales stayed flat owing at least in part to the US government shutdown.

The company's North American market is most significant, bringing in over half of its net sales in FY25 (see Pg 2 of the link for details). As a Reuters report points out, "Delayed payments for food assistance in October and November hurt lower-income households that were already struggling to make ends meet amid high prices and a tepid labor market". The company confirmed that sales in December 2025 were indeed affected for this reason.

...but supportive factors are in play too

How big the impact of higher oil prices would be still remains to be seen. The EIA's latest forecast expects that crude prices on average will rise by just USD 10/bbl in 2026, a far cry from the spike witnessed in the past couple of weeks. Even otherwise, though, the extent of P&G's sales softening can be protected for two reasons:

  • In its last earnings call, the company pointed out that "The balance of the company grew organic sales nearly 3% with almost all regions outside the U.S. growing or accelerating in the quarter." Even though an oil price spike will have a global impact, growth in other regions can potentially provide some buffer to the company's numbers.
  • The Fed projects that the US economy will grow by 2.3% in 2026, slightly up from 2.2% in 2025. This can change in light of recent events, of course, but it's worth noting that even now, forecasters are positive on the economy's prospects. The Philly Fed's latest quarterly survey of professional forecasters reveals expectations of a 2.5% growth. If growth softens, but still stays on trend or above trend levels of 2.2%, P&G's demand might not be impacted meaningfully.

How margins support

Unlike in the government shutdown, though, there's another source of concern for P&G right now. And that's rising costs, which in turn can impact margins. In my recent article on the latest inflation release, I pointed out that there could be upward inflationary pressure by 0.5 percentage points in the US this year, which could have a bearing on costs.

Already, a softening in margins has been observed. In Q2 FY26, the non-GAAP operating margin dropped to 25.5% (Q2 FY25: 26.2%). Similarly, the non-GAAP net margin was at 20.6% (Q2 FY25: 21.1%).

To be fair, though, the margins are still quite strong. This is most evident when the stock is compared with the company's closest peers, Colgate-Palmolive (CL) and Unilever (UL). P&G's operating income margin over the past twelve months is at 25.6%, compared to CL at 20.8% and UL at 20.25%. This gives the ability to absorb higher costs while still maintaining relatively high margins compared with peers.

Favourable market multiples

Still, with core EPS growth at just 2% in H1 FY26, here I work with the assumption that the company's forecast midpoint growth of 2% for FY26 won't be met. Instead, it's assumed that the core EPS will remain unchanged from FY25. The stock's forward non-GAAP P/E lands at 22x as a result; compared to its five-year average P/E of 24.2x, this indicates close to 10% upside to the stock even now.

Further, the company has encouraging longer-term expectations for core EPS growth. As the slide below shows, it expects growth to accelerate to "mid-to-high single digits". Assuming that the midpoint of the range is 7%, the EPS would rise to USD 9.8 by FY31. This brings the forward P/E to 15.3x and indicates a price upside of over 50% by then.

Low volatility, dependable dividends

The stock's defensive qualities are worth noting as well. Its 24 month beta value is at just 0.16, indicating that even if the stock markets fluctuate, it can stay steady, even if it doesn't exactly offer the highest price returns.

Its steady dividends also acquire greater appeal at a time when price returns in the stock markets are uncertain. Assuming that the company increases dividends by 5% in April, much like it did last year, the dividend would amount to USD 4.3 for FY26 and the forward dividend yield would be 2.8%. This is lower than the staples' sector median of 3.3%, but there's something to be said for the company's consistent payout over 69 years, and their growth for as many as well.

What's next?

In the current scenario, I believe that PG is due to make gains. Sure, there are genuine risks to its sales and earnings performance. The macro situation can be a drag even on staple stocks, as the company's performance following the US government shutdown shows.

But there are supporting factors at play too. Key among these is margins. Even if sales and earnings growth softens, the company's strong margins compared with peers provide a crucial buffer. Also, the company's longer-term outlook is undeniably positive. The stock's favourable valuations, predictable dividend payouts, and low price volatility also work in its favour. PG is a buy for me now.

data.title: goeasy's Investment Thesis Got Crushed Overnight, Don't Buy The Dip
data.description:
goeasy (GSY:CA) (EHMEF), a non-prime lender based in Canada, shocked many investors on March 10 when it released a pre-earnings financial update that was much worse than people expected. The stock crashed 57% on the day of the announcement and has fallen 69% in total since March 9, as the investment thesis got crushed overnight.

I'll go over the update in more detail later on, but in short:

  • Charge-offs (loans that are written off) and net increases in allowance for credit losses are going to be higher than expected in Q4.
  • The company's accounting practices proved to be too aggressive.
  • goeasy withdrew its three-year outlook while suspending dividends and buybacks.

As someone who first bought goeasy six years ago during the pandemic crash and first covered goeasy in 2020 and last covered it in 2024, this is extremely disappointing. My last article from November 2024 was titled "goeasy: Why Double-Digit Annual Returns Are Likely," but it's clear that the title should have stated "negative double-digit annual returns" instead.

Now, many of you are probably wondering whether you should buy the dip or not. My answer, personally, is a big fat "no." In fact, I think it's a sell.

Dips can make sense to buy when the price drops while the thesis is still intact, but the thesis changed. This used to be a dividend stock with a relatively high yield and double-digit dividend and EPS growth as well. Now, it's more of a turnaround play, with a potential turnaround being too far out and highly uncertain.

While the company mentioned a 6-point action plan to strengthen the business, management's credibility has taken a hit, and it's hard to reliably estimate how that action plan will turn out.

In the meantime, there aren't any real catalysts to push the stock higher, and we still have to hear what management has to say during the Q4 earnings call after it reports earnings on March 25.

Unless your investing/trading style is buying into turnaround plays very early or hoping for short-term oversold bounces, holding onto GSY:CA stock doesn't make sense to me. I give it a sell rating. Save your dip-buying money for easier opportunities.

The News That Caused goeasy To Go South

The company started its press release by saying that it "expects to incur an incremental charge-off in Q4 2025 of approximately $178M against gross consumer loans receivable of $5.5B as of December 31, 2025, and a related write-down of approximately $55M for loan interest and fees. Total Company net charge-offs (including the incremental charge-off above) in the quarter are expected to be approximately $331M."

Following that statement, goeasy mentioned that its allowance for credit losses on gross consumer loans receivable will be $86 million higher in Q4 than they were in Q3 2025.

For the full year, its net charge-off rate is expected to be ~12.9%. For context, its annualized net charge-off rate was 9.1% in Q4 2025 and 8.8% in Q4 2024.

The company then withdrew its Q4 outlook and its three-year forecast. The three-year forecast was quite optimistic, since it forecasted growth, 23%+ returns on equity from 2025-2027, increasing operating margins, and net charge-offs in the high-single digits. It makes complete sense why the stock fell so much after it withdrew this optimistic guidance without any prior warning.

Another killer was the suspension of the buyback plan and the dividend. The annualized dividend used to be $5.84/share. Before the crash, that meant the yield was over 5%. And since the company generally announced dividend increases along with its Q4 results, I'm sure many people were expecting a dividend increase, not a complete suspension.

But What Actually Caused The Financials To Crumble?

goeasy's LendCare business, which it acquired in 2021, was the main culprit. LendCare provides financing solutions mostly in the auto and powersports categories, but it's not going so well right now. The $178 million in expected incremental charge-off is due to loans from this company. Management expects LendCare's poor results to also lead to an annual net charge-off rate in the mid-teens this year before improving in 2027 and beyond.

On top of that, management is going to revise LendCare's reporting practices.

Specifically, "The historical reporting practice resulted in certain customer payments being recorded as received while they were in fact in the process of being settled at month end, some of which were ultimately not collected, and also impacted the company's reported delinquencies."

Apparently, the impact of this correction won't be material to goeasy's results, but this doesn't exactly make me trust the company.

6-Point Action Plan Isn't Enough To Make Me Buy

Here's management's 6-point plan, directly from its press release, that it believes will strengthen the business:

  • Focusing growth on easyfinancial's unsecured and home equity lending direct-to-consumer business
  • Reducing auto and powersports originations from LendCare's merchant channels
  • Integrating easyfinancial and LendCare into one unified operating model
  • Implementing identified operational efficiencies expected to deliver approximately $30M in annualized cost savings
  • Strengthening leadership at LendCare, including appointing Farhan Ali Khan as the new Head of LendCare
  • Not repurchasing shares under its Normal Course Issuer Bid and suspending its quarterly dividend, effective immediately, until further notice

Everything in the plan makes sense. goeasy wants to focus more on its core business while reducing certain LendCare originations. It also wants to cut costs, strengthen LendCare leadership, and preserve capital by suspending the dividend and buybacks.

I'm not hating on the plan, but there are a few questions in my head: how well can goeasy execute this plan? How long will it take to play out? And why should shareholders trust the company after what just happened?

I Sold As Soon As I Read The News

As soon as I saw the goeasy news, I sold the stock. I didn't hesitate. I'm thankful I only had a small position of 9 shares. I knew to keep it small because I'm generally risk-averse with most financial stocks, as things like this can happen quickly, so I always take that into consideration.

What's funny is that I had been patiently watching GSY:CA stock fall for a while (I had sold it near its highs in September 2025), and I finally re-entered with a small position on March 4 at a price of $106.71. Initially, that seemed to be a good price because it was a swing low on the chart, and the stock had a 5%+ dividend yield with a positive three-year outlook.

But when the company's credibility, future outlook, and dividend were thrown out the window, I said to myself, "What reason do I have to hold onto this stock?" I found no reason, and essentially sold right away at $62.57. With the stock at $35.72 now, at least I saved myself from further losses.

Earnings Estimates Got Hammered

Right now, you may look at goeasy's EPS estimates and forward valuation and conclude that this is a great value play. If analysts' estimates are correct, then the stock trades at just 3.4x its expected 2027 earnings, and when (if) the company reinstates its dividend, you'd likely be sitting on a high yield. That can happen, and that's essentially the bull case from here.

However, the main issue is that we don't know how reliable these estimates really are, especially this early on in the turnaround process.

If you look at the EPS revision trend, you'll see just how much the estimates have been reset. Just one month ago, the 2025, 2026, and 2027 EPS estimates were $16.33, $20.09, and $23.59, respectively.

Now, they're $3.46, $5.07, and $10.31, respectively.

Analysts were way off the first time, and they can be way off again.

My Final Thoughts On GSY:CA Stock

We all make mistakes in the stock market. The key point is to not let a mistake destroy your account. Keep risky positions at a manageable size, and get out when the thesis changes. Don't keep holding in hopes of a turnaround that you can't predict when there are thousands of other stocks out there to choose from. Of course, this is just my opinion, not professional advice.

I'm going to pay attention to what management says in its upcoming earnings report, and I'll look for improvements in the coming quarters, especially regarding EPS revisions. I want to see there be a "floor" for EPS expectations because we have no idea what that floor is right now.

Either way, with the dividend gone and my trust in the company gone as well, I say farewell, goeasy. It's been a nice run for the past 6 years being in and out of the stock, but it's time to move on to better companies.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CEF Market Weekly Review: Daily-NAV CEFs Point To More Pain For CLO Equity
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up - highlighting individual fund news and events - as well as the top-down - providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the first week of March. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was a difficult week for CEFs, with all sectors finishing in the red. EM funds led to the downside.

Fixed-income sector discounts weakened more than equity sector CEFs over the last week.

The last bout of marked anxiety pushed the median CEF sector discount past its longer-term average.

Market Themes

CLO Equity has been under pressure over the past year. This was despite relatively well-behaved credit markets, even if there was an odd cockroach or two now and again.

Our estimates suggest that February may be a particularly difficult month for the sector (nearly all CEFs in the sector report NAVs on a monthly basis and are yet to report the February numbers). Current estimates of February NAVs in our CEF Tool are a double-digit drop. These estimates are derived from a regression relationship with XFLT which does report on a daily basis and holds about a third of the portfolio in CLO Equity. For example, this is what the monthly NAV changes look like for XFLT and OCCI.

With February being the worst month for XFLT for a long time, we are worried about the fallout across the broader CLO Equity CEF space. Of course there could be an issue idiosyncratic to XFLT such as specific portfolio positioning; however, ARDC, which has a 10% CLO Equity allocation, also had one of its worst NAV months as well in February. The common theme appears to be weakness in the broader syndicated loan market, precipitated by the AI disruption theme of the software sector.

Ultimately, both the tightness in loan spreads (i.e., high loan prices) and volatility in loan prices that we have seen over the last few weeks, particularly in the software sector, are headwinds for the sector. An environment of wide loan spreads amid modest volatility / low defaults where CLO Equity could shine may not be on the cards for some time, though it’s impossible to tell, and we could see a bounceback next month.

Until the recent decline in loan prices, the primary catalyst for CLO Equity underperformance has been what are called captive CLO funds. Because these funds internalize fees, they can print CLOs at a less favorable arbitrage level than would be accepted by third-party CLO Equity managers. This means that captive fund activity puts significant downward pressure on loan spreads and reduces the income of CLO Equity securities which are highly sensitive to this due to their annuity-like profile.

If CLO Equity funds do post bad February NAVs, price behavior will likely be very messy, likely driving fund prices to new lows. The extent and relentlessness of the CLO Equity sell-off mean that even funds like XFLT and EIC which have minority allocations to the sector, will not remain immune. It also means that it’s hard to maintain conviction in the sector, as there are no obvious limits to how long the sell-off can carry on. This is different from traditional credit assets, which have well-tested historical ranges of credit spreads. It’s also different from a sector like BDCs, where performance has remained strong despite poor news flow.

Market Commentary

It was another quiet month for CEF distributions. There were the usual small adjustments for BlackRock ECAT and BCAT. Invesco bond fund VLT slightly cut the distribution, which it also did around 6 months ago. The distribution of $0.0915 remains well above NII of around $0.062 as of the last semi-annual report. NII will rise over the next few months to reflect the lower cost of leverage via lower short-term rates (offset partly by the decline in income on its loan assets).

A couple of John Hancock funds, JHS and JHI cut the distribution by double-digit levels. The distributions of these funds have been all over the place, and the cuts are a reversal of the jump in the previous quarter.

Stance and Takeaways

On the back of XFLT's February struggles, we exited our allocation to both it as well as EIC. We have long steered clear of full CLO Equity CEFs for a number of factors, including their historically rich valuations, likely distribution cuts, and a tendency to deleverage. However, the sheer extent of the sell-off in CLO Equity has left a mark even on partial CLO Equity funds as well, such as XFLT and EIC. For now, the structural shifts in the CLO industry as well as weakness in the syndicated loan market remain key headwinds for the space, with little if any light at the end of the tunnel.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Principal Well-Being Index: Firms Looking Through The Sticker Shock
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist | Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist

Price pressures remain top of mind for many firms, fueling the desire to improve profitability through increased business efficiency. As a result, firms have signaled borrowing activity this year will skew towards funding technology upgrades, productivity enhancements, and expansion plans, despite elevated financing costs. This willingness to invest in strategically critical imperatives is reshaping capital allocation decisions.

What initiatives firms will use financing to support this year

Principal Well-Being Index

The latest Principal Well-Being Index indicates that price pressures remain a central concern for businesses. This is prompting firms to focus on extracting efficiency gains, particularly through technological innovation.

The Principal Well-Being Index measures sentiment among small- and medium-sized businesses by asking business decision-makers about current conditions and the economic outlook. The most recent survey, conducted between January 15 and February 1, collected responses from 1,000 firms—on par with the NFIB Small Business Optimism Index in scale.

Results show that the cost of healthcare, employee benefits, and tariffs on foreign goods and materials remain significant concerns across firms of all sizes. In response, businesses are restraining job openings and keeping headcount broadly stable. Simultaneously, many are actively pursuing efficiency improvements, including borrowing to fund technology upgrades, productivity enhancements, and expansion plans, even as financing costs remain elevated. Notably, there is a growing sense of urgency: firms across sectors and size categories report a willingness to borrow at interest rates approaching 12% when investments are viewed as strategically critical.

That willingness points to a structural shift. The imperative to invest in growth while extracting efficiency gains through technology is reshaping capital allocation decisions. As a result, both small and large businesses are increasingly prepared to take on debt to finance investments in software, automation, and digital infrastructure, despite borrowing costs that remain well above pre-pandemic levels.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
